Beginning it's twice a day after the, the next period it's, it's once a day. Role play again, live calls, notice day four. So we get people up pretty quick. Now that's contingent on them passing. If they don't pass, we keep them in there training until they're up. So if somebody asked earlier, how do you not blow through leads? Well, we don't let them talk to leads until they pass the test. And even then when someone starts and I'll cover this in a second and I want you guys to ask the questions I know you're going to ask when I say this, it doesn't matter. Remember I said that we have savages. Everybody who's coming in is number one at their place. If you come here, you start at the bottom. Now you can have a corner mark, which is that if you hit above the KPI, we can pull you over to the closer team. Let's say if we hired a closer, but if you can't set, you can't close. It's if you can dodge a wrench, you can dodge a ball. It's a hundred percent the same thing. If you can't get somebody to set an appointment, you're not getting them to give you money. That's for sure. Is that all the same for B2B? We're dude, we're B2B. Bro, like we're B2B. Okay. So yes, we have everyone set before they close. You cannot close unless you set. And it's also because that gives you an appreciation as a closer for the work that went in to give you this lead, which is why our team, all closers, if they have a gap in their calendar, every closer is expected to keep two hours a day of outbound, because it keeps the sword sharp and it keeps them appreciating what it does. And the guys who are savages, they'll have eight hours of inbound. They'll do four hours of outbound because they want to win because that's the only advantage because everybody gets about the same leads. So the only advantage they can get on each other is how much outbound they can drum up for themselves. Okay. So how do you pass phase fours that you meet live colx standards? So at this point, we give them half calendars. Yep. So we give them half calendars. Yeah. So day four to 14, if they passed all the tests, so we, once you've done that, they get a half calendar for sets. And as long as they're doing well, then they get full and then they kick KPI and then we pull them over. Okay. So some of these might be like, why not just have them read the script, Jonathan, because you'll sound like you're reading a fucking script. Man, I got on the phone with this sales guy sound like you read a script. Yeah. Cause he was reading it. That's why he sounded like he was reading it. If you know the script, if you breed the script, you don't have that problem. Would you guys like to know how to memorize the script? Here's how it works. You print out two copies of the script. You read the script out loud. You use a marker and you black out one random word on the script. And then you read it out loud again and you let your memory fill in the gap with the one random word. And then you continue again and you black out another word and you read it again until you've blacked out the entire script. And beyond that, it's shadowing and listening to game tape every single day until you close. By the way, that is how you, that is how you memorize anything. By the way, if you were like, how do I memorize something? That's how you do it. Okay. Now, why don't you like, I want you to talk about role-playing like if there's one thing that I want you to take away from training sales, it's learning how to do role play. If you are not doing role play with your team, I don't even know what we're doing. I don't even know how you train sales with that role play. It's just hoping at that point. It's just hoping they're going to figure it out. So once you talk about it, you can go for it. And the thing with role play is most people do it wrong. And I did it wrong for the longest time. And so the traditional way to role play is you have, let's say I'm the closer, you're the prospect, and we're going back and forth. And as the manager, like this is the wrong way. So don't do it this way. This is the way that I started doing it. And then after, after I learned how to do the right way, like drastically compressed time and how quickly I could get a rep up to speed. And so your prospects, I'm salesperson, we're going back and forth. And then the manager lets a whole conversation play by. So they let the whole conversation play by, say we're practicing the discovery portion. And then at the end of the discovery, the manager is like, okay, you know, you did that thing really well. You did this thing really well, but next time do it like this. And then the next time they have the opportunity to try that thing is maybe like five days later. And so the learning does not stick. And you also, you're at a such, such a slow iteration and feedback phase where you're going to be in the feeling that I have with sales reps when I train this way is I'd walk by them on a sales call. And I'd be like, why are they still saying that thing like that? But then I realized, well, I never train them because I was trying to hammer all these other things home. So the right way and the way we like to do it, and this has helped me tremendously, is in reps, when they first experience this, they freak out, so you have to set the expectation is we interrupt them so many different times. And so when I'm training with a sales rep, number one, I want to show them they're doing a good job. And so I'm lean forward. Let's say I'm on zoom, my face is forward, my eyes are big, I'm smiling as they're following the process. The process is everything. And so I'm like, yep, nice job. Great job. Nice job there. Great job there. And so I'm reinforcing them doing the following the process, following the script. Now, once they go off script, that's where I'm saying, Hey, stop right there, go back, try to get like this. And then I model it. Hey, do you mind if I ask you a question? Go back, say it exactly like that. Go, you did it. Great. One more time. Great. One more time. Got it. You knocked it. You locked it in. And so when we're first training a sales rep, we will sit there and we'll interrupt them so many different times. And so I'll literally be sitting there, interrupt them 50, 60, 70 times throughout one session, because I'm just slowly chipping away and slowly correcting what they're doing modeling. I never tell them they do it wrong. I stop them. I show them how to do it right. That part's so important. You show them how you model it for them. And then you go back, you do it again, you do it again, you do it again, and you lock it in. And so by going through this process, it's extremely painful. It takes a ton of energy, but it's so worth it because you're able to pass information and train so much faster than doing it the other way. And so we do this every single day, every morning. And one small tactical way is we always rotate through the different pieces of the clothes. And so if you have a week, it's intro discovery pitch clothes, we're going through those different parts. And it's important because we had actually one of our portfolio companies, they're like, hey, our team got really bad at the discovery process. And we're listening to the calls. I'm like, okay, wait, what are you guys training in the morning? And they're like, we just train the clothes every single day. And I'm like, okay, let's, let's, let's make sure we're hitting each part every day. And so like role play is we've done all sorts of training role play is like the most valuable way and you just got to make sure you do it right. Yeah, Kevin, if the rep isn't coachable, I mean, we screen for that on the front end. But if someone, honestly, like, we have a team of ego list killers, like you need someone who has that deep insecurity, like not surface level, it's got to be like deep like surface level and security, they come out in their air again, they have egos and then, and then they're useless, you need like the deep kind that like, they will do whatever it takes to win. That's what you need. And so that's what we have. So I'll give you a quick, quick, quick skin here. So role playing, you act out the sales convo one person plays the prospect, the other person plays selling them, the prospect gives the same problems that you'd actually get on real calls, your job is to respond using the correct scripts and techniques, right? And so the steps that we do, like Jacob said, and we're hitting it from different different voices so that this sinks in for you. Because if I can get you to do this for your sales teams, you maybe for yourself, if you're the only person, you will sell better. So number one is you frame it. So Jacob said, he frames it before manager explains what to practice, how to do it and why say we're talking about disco today. The point of disco is this, here's how it sounds. Great, let's do it. So he models it, he shows it. And then he says, great, you copy me now. Now you practice it via role play with fast feedback loops. And he says, great, we'll keep doing it until you get it right. And then once you get it right, we'll have you do it again. So that we lock it in. That's, you know, that's the terminology we use. And just be grateful that you actually get the opportunity to have a team that actually knows how to teach it, right? Then you'll recap the role play and move on to the next person. So we're doing disco, it'll be disco with this person, disco with this person, disco with this person, the sales manager is really sales trainer. That's what they are, like they train sales. And to be clear, being a sales trainer, not necessarily the same skill set as being a closer, it's a different skill set. And so sometimes there are guys who can do both, some guys can do one or the other. So it's entirely different. And so the test here is that before someone starts on the phone, they have to pass tone, recaps and objection looping. Once they pass each test, then they can go live. Now, shadowing really obvious, you just, it's a ride along. So you get to see how the day to day goes. It's also a way for the guys to get to know each other on the team, just build camaraderie early on. So that's why we do shadowing. Beyond that, game tape, which you saw on the calendar there, it's where we study call recordings, right? So you'll start specific, and then you expand as you master the script. And so you have your script with you, they have, they should be, because if your team all follows this, then the calls sound like the script. You can imagine how dumb this would be if we're like, hey, listen to game tape, and then they're holding the script and they're listening to game tape, and they're like, this, that's nothing to do with this. And then they think, oh, this must be trash, guys, what these guys are doing, that's why maintaining this stuff is so important. All right, let's see here. All right, so that's how we get people going, right? And then once they're proactive, and we get, you know, 14 days, then we transition to ongoing training. And so I want to make the point here, though, is that like, we're doing two days before this, they're doing nothing but training for 14 days. The last four to 14 is if they pass the test, they're doing half days, they got to hit KPI, then they get full days. And then once they're at, you know, full days, they're, they're functionally out. But the thing is that most people then say, well, I don't want to train sales anymore, because I don't want to take an hour out of my guy's day or 30 minutes out of my guy's day. Michael Jordan was less likely to miss a practice than a game. Like that is the, that is the point here, right? And so we have to have the culture of ongoing training from a consistent basis. And so for our guys, if we have hunting kill mode, this is a normal, a normal calendar day, they would wake up, I want you to go through the calendar. Yep. So when we, when we look at the calendar, we want to like first off the importance of the calendars, we want to make sure they know exactly what to do at each part of the day. And so like we've, we've, we've in the past had teams and it's like, they don't know what to do. So then they just do nothing. And so this calendar gives them a guide of exactly what to do and what they hit. And so in the morning, the first thing they do when they get into the office is they're reviewing, they're reviewing notes and they're making new notes. And so that's where they're prepping for their calls. They're getting ready for the calls that they have for the day. Then really from there, they go into their priority list. And so this is looking at, did I nurture all my leads? Did I, did I hit all my pipelines, all my followups that I need to hit? Am I working my outbound? And so whenever I don't have a call, I'm going through that list and I'm making sure I'm hitting each of those pieces. Then we jump into close calls. And so you got calls on the calendar. Most valuable for you to think to do is close those calls from there. You see, we have the training block, we jump into the training. That's where we're hitting role plays. And then throughout the whole day, you continue to rinse and repeat. As you can see there, you have your outbound blocks. It's important as you scale the team, you just stagger those outbound blocks. You don't kill all of your availability during that time and you just rinse and repeat. And so every single day you jump in, you know exactly what you're doing. You're working through those blocks. And then if you have nothing, you're hitting your priority list. So if you're like, I mean, I saw someone say like, do you guys actually wake, work six hours a day, sorry, six days a week, 12 hours a day? The honest truth is yes. Our guys do. I don't know how to say that differently. Like I got here this morning at 4.50 and there were three sales guys here. And by the time like six, like by the time six rolls around, they're all here. You know what I mean? So sometimes if they have like, you know, maybe a slightly later call, they'll come in, they'll still come in that time, they'll just work out. And then they'll hit calls. Sometimes they have a little bit dead time in the middle of the day. So they'll just work out then. Like that's, that's kind of the difference. The key point that I want you guys to take away from here is that everyone hits their priority list. Everyone hits their notes in the morning. All the time that they have left over is close calls and outbound block. And then the one block that goes ball up, the only thing that you're allowed to skip training for is if you have a close call that goes long, that time is blocked so that close calls can't come in. But if you have a call that goes long from beforehand, this is the only time that you can be late to training. All right. So I'll just walk through daily team training quickly, which is train 30 minutes a day Monday to Friday, onsite and remote. Again, we do shout outs and testimonials, hop in on it earlier. The reason we do this is that shout outs reinforce the team and reinforce good behavior because we want to give them status for doing the right stuff. Right? How do you create culture? This is how you do it. So we give them like, be nice about, you know, give them shout outs. Testimonials from the customers because we want to have conviction. Right? Like the number one thing, like if you want to have really fixed people's tone and sell the right way, have them believe in the product. It's the easiest way to do it. And how do you get people to believe in the product? Give them proof. So just like we have to sell the customers, we have to sell the team, we have to do it every day. Like conviction is not, is a, is a consumable thing. It has to be replenished. We obviously then train the script, which we talked about breaking the four parts, intro disco offer pitch and sorry, intro disco offer and looping. And then we have a hot topic, which is if there's something that the team is struggling with on Friday, manager will just do that. And so that's what it looks like in terms of day to day. And weekly team training. Once a week, we do a 60 minute, you want to go for this? Just yep. Yep. So once a week, basically we're going through, so a lot of this is just more around like game tape. And so we start the meeting the same way. We're going through shout outs. We're reinforcing the behaviors. And then from there, the game tape, other than role play, game tape is the most valuable thing you can do. And so that's where we're selecting three to five of like the most recent calls. And then the manager is telling you what to look for and why. And so this is so important because like, we don't want to just watch game tape mindlessly and not have any takeaways for it. So it's like, Hey, we're looking for this. This is why we're looking for it. Let's rock. The team observes the game tape. They're all listening. And then the manager will model the proper technique. And so, Hey, instead of doing this next time, we want to do this. They'll model it and then we'll go around and we'll role play that with each team member until everyone nails it. We'll recap what the takeaway was, and then we'll rinse and repeat. And so we just continue to do this three to five calls each time. Then we go into weekly one-on-ones. So really with this, the big thing that we start with is like, how are you doing? Like, how are you feeling one to 10? Where are you at? How's life? We want to make sure they're doing all right because sometimes the reason they're not closing deals is because their girlfriend just broke up with them. So like, we want to check in on them as a person. Then we're talking about short and long-term goals. And so short-term goals is more so focused and on the sale of skills itself. And so this is where you're saying, Hey, and we like to say one for the week. So this is your one for the week. We've listened to game tape, the biggest leverage point, the biggest constraint within your sales process is this piece right here. And so one thing that I like to do with the one for the week is have them write it on a sticky note. And then this sticky note goes in front of their desk. They're always looking at it. This is the biggest thing they're focusing on. This is the biggest unlock that they can have for their sales. The next is the long-term goals. And so this is so, so important for retaining reps. And so like at the very beginning, we'll stay away from long-term goals until they get proficient. And then we want to start talking about what are their long-term career goals? And then what can we focus and on to help move that forward? And so with this, like let's say a rep has been with us for a while and we're like, okay, they want to go into sales management. What are the skills that we have to build to become a sales manager? And so like Alex said, it's like a big part of a sales manager is being a great sales coach. That's like the main role of the job. And so maybe we'll hand off some of the morning meetings to them. They take the lead. They're the one giving the feedback. They're the one running the role play. And then afterwards we're giving them the feedback on that specific thing. And so really with this, I mean, again, if they're below standard, we're not focusing on the long-term goal. Because they might not be here. They might not be here. Yes. And we want to get them, we also don't want to dilute their focus. And so like we want to make sure they're nailing their core job. And then from there, it's looking at the different skills. Where can we develop them? And how do we grow them into a leader? So two things that I saw. One is someone said, how could I even do this with three reps? It's the same thing. Like you have a weekly one-on-one. We're talking one 30-minute meeting. You've got a weekly 60-minute meetings. That's 90 minutes in total. And then you have a daily touch base in the morning. That's the, or you can do the middle of the day if you want, depending on what your hot time is for sales calls for your market. Like for us, we're like middle of the day because that like our for us, our mornings are super heavy on calls. So middle of the day is the best time for us. And so that's basically it. Like that's the only team, or like only rep with manager requirements. The rest is on the rep. So it's really like three things. It's not that that's what it is. So that's that. We already covered short and long to worlds. We're good there. Okay. So the other thing that someone said was, I think it was Adam Jacobs. He said, anyone else here feel like they need to like blow everything up and like rebuild from the foundation? The honest truth is that's like the uncomfortable reality for a lot of, honestly, a lot of businesses is that like, when you really like see through the matrix, you realize that everything was wrong to begin with, which is that you're like, oh, I can't scale this because I have no team. And the culture I have sucks. And that's not just sales. That's everything. And so every department, I mean, especially as you get bigger, you'll have departments. It's like departments will have their own cultures in and of themselves. And I have to clean cultures up all the time. It sucks. And that's always going to be predicated on the strength of the leader. And the leader creates the culture. So how do you create a culture? So let's actually like, let's operationalize this. So what is culture? So culture is the rules that govern reinforcement in an organization said differently, rule, if this action, this reinforcer, if you do this, this happens. So reinforcer reason to do it again. So I'll put a plug these definitions in it. Culture equals, if you do this action, will give you this reason to do it again in an organization. And how many of those do you have in the organization? Organizations have hundreds of rules. Most of them are unspoken. So on the sales team, there's an unspoken rule that the guys work out. They don't have to work out, but it's part of the culture. The guys work out, they want to be healthy. They want to, they want to win, right? It's like, it's unspoken. We haven't had to say you have to be here at a certain time. They just see the leaderboard and see themselves at the bottom and know that if they're at the bottom, they're not going to be here. So they show up earlier because they know if they're at the bottom, they're not going to be here. If this, then that. So then they work really hard. Right. And so like, can you be late to meetings in some companies? Yes. In other companies, if you're late to meetings, you're fired. And so culture is just all the rules spoken and unspoken about what is allowed and what is praised, what is tolerated in a business. And so right now, if your standards, if you're like, man, I, I want to change the culture, but these people won't listen to me, what do you do? You let them go. Either they change their behavior or they leave. And that means that you might have a short-term decrease in business so that you can fix the culture of the team. And then once you do fix the culture of the team, you have to protect that culture and not let a cancerous person in. Once the culture gets big enough though, like it's, it's so obvious when someone comes in, they either adapt and join into the culture or they just get completely spit out like a cancer. It's super fast. And the stronger you make that culture, the stronger it happens. So the question that I would ask you guys are, what are the rules that govern your sales team? Like you have rules right now. Maybe one of your rules is if you close really well, you don't have to follow the script. That might be one of your unspoken rules. If you, if you're, if you're above KPI, you don't have to show up to training. That might be one of your rules. If you, you know, if you close a lot, you get to complain about lead quality. That might be one of your rules. If you close really well, you don't have to listen to the boss anymore. Maybe that's one of your rules. It's literally whatever you tolerate. Those are the rules. Maybe one of the rules is if you have a month of bad sales, we still give you leads. That might be one of your rules. And so you have to think about what are the things that I'm reinforcing or allowing or tolerating that tell people, because we have to think about the net reinforcement. If I can get paid and not work, that is a large reinforcer. Just a reason to do it. Right. And so we have to make sure that all the incentives are aligned and incentives, everyone just thinks about compensation. Most of the incentives are not not compensation based, which is why I'll briefly talk about comp, but I have culture is what will drive the team, not the comp. I'm just telling you right now, if comp drives the team, it's a bad culture. I have teams in this company right now. I have one team comp drives the culture. It's I got to clean it up. And so again, it's like weeding a garden. You're going to weed it and you're going to clean it up and you're going to put good flowers in there, but you got to keep weeding it because weeds will come up. People get lazy. People slack off. It's like that's where the culture is so important. And so training for us, what are the rules for us? Training, practice, timeliness, work ethic, process above everything, no pre-medanas, zero eagle, and everyone starts at the bottom. That's what it is. How hard is the changing a mediocre team? It's easy if you're willing to let your sales go down. You just get the mediocre people out. Like you just want maniacs. You just want people who want to get better. If you have that, you can win. So last words on culture, you might need to fire half your mediocre team to build a world-class one. People need to think they need to find stars because they don't know how to make them. And every salesperson who joins our team, like I said, was number one on their team and not one has ever come in at number one. So that is our culture stuff. Appreciate you. So we talked about show rate improvement. We talked about lead qualification. I'll go back one. So we did lead qualification and nurture. We talked about show rate improvement. We did BW sales strategies. We talked about sales scripts and training. We did close rate conversion. We did objection handling. We did sales team management and training and culture. I did briefly talk about upselling. We did video sales letter and VSCLs. We talked about follow up and re-engagement. We talked about disco. We talked about speed of lead. And yeah. So this is the last piece here, which is the getting more salespeople. Oh yeah, comp. Good call. Sorry. I'll talk about that real quick and then I'll get into the getting more salespeople. Okay. So compensation. My compensation philosophy for salespeople after doing this for a while, two things right off the bat. If you have a self-gen team, meaning if you're like home services and people go out, knock doors, and they bring in business, different monster, that's going to be pure commission. It's basically cack. It's fully loaded cack because you have no marketing spend. It's just whatever you pay that guy is what it costs to get a customer. So that's why those guys get paid the most because it's one of the hardest jobs. On the other hand, if you do any kind of lead gen of any kind, whether it's content, you have an outreach team, like that does like digital outreach and like, you know, enriches leads and sets and closes all that stuff. Or you run paid ads that people go through a funnel in order to buy your stuff. Any of, or you have affiliates that send you business, whatever. If you have any other type of lead gen where they're just closing leads that you're generating, then I like a 50-50 split between salary and commission. Okay. Now I got that from Vista as well, the private equity firm, because they did a bunch of big analysis and I just believe them and it has worked well for me. If you go pure commission with those guys, it's too commission breathy and it's very hard for these guys to like have stability in their life. If you have a bad month, you don't make commission, it's like you can't have the job. And so what I want to do is have them be able to pay their basic needs on the base and then they live their lifestyle on the other half. Okay. Now this then levels up to OTE, which means on target earnings. Okay. So every sales role should have an on target earnings. If this person converts the amount of leads that they should be converting, they should make this amount of money. Okay. Now what is that? How do I break that into comp though? So I'm going to set the comp based on what I believe the level of skill that is required to attract this level of person. This actually works for anything, not just sales, but for sales, it's like, I think that the sale of this caliber requires this caliber sales person, which means that the more process oriented your sales process is, the less skill the sales person needs, which means the less you have to pay sales people, because it's the less skill that they have to come in with, which drives more profitability for the business, which allows you to reinvest, grow, scale, et cetera. So if you're somebody who has no process whatsoever, no marketing skill whatsoever, then you need a rainmaker to basically drag someone from completely unaware to close because they're exceptionally good at sales. If you have an amazing brand, an amazing education process, and a very amazing offer, you just need someone to say, do you want it? And then you just transact. And so if I have the difference between those two people, could be multiple hundreds of thousands of dollars a year. And so a lot of people ask me, what is the perfect comp structure? It just 100% comes down to, can you attract people who are at the level of talent at that price point? Basically it. Can I put this comp out and attract the level of talent I need to fill this role? That's it. The only nugget that I will give you on comp besides the 50-50 piece is that the only true commission change, because it's like, should I do it off of percentages? Should I do it off? I don't care. It actually doesn't matter because I just want you to do these actions and you could do it off of total deals. You could do total deal volume. You can do it 100 different ways. But I will say that the thing that has made a difference is if I can tie the commission to cash collected upfront, I would say it's the only change that I've made on Sales Team Comp that reliably increases closed quality and pools cash forward, which I see as a positive thing. Now Dan, you said, can you give a range? It's so tough to give a range because if you're closing, think of the hypothetical extreme. If you're a real estate agent, you're getting 6% of a building that's $20 million. That's what you get for the sale. On the other extreme, if you're selling car washes and you've got 200 cars a day walking past you and you're trying to upsell them by a couple bucks a piece, you might get a dollar on an upsell. But you got 100 at bats. It so widely depends on the skill, the nature of the transaction, the ticket size of the product, the process that the person has to go in order to close sales. If I just stand here and cars drive by and I have to say the same six lines, not a lot of skill. If I have to take an enterprise and get six decision makers to buy a three-year licensing of my enterprise software for $500,000 a year, it's going to require more skill. I'll say generically speaking, and again, it also depends on markets. I will give you the ugliest estimation I can. SDRs, what do we pay? 60 all in? 75,000 on target earnings. That's 50-50 base in variable. That's for SDRs, so sales development reps, setters. Closers, OT is what, 120? 150. OT is 150. Then if you have a super specialized closer, they're going to be 200, 250, sometimes 400. It just depends on how specialized they are, how expensive the thing is, and in some ways, what are the stakes of losing? Again, a lot of it still comes down to how replaceable are they? There might be high stakes, but if there's somebody else who can do it, somebody else can do it. Hopefully that helped with that. Getting more salespeople now. Tori, hopefully that helped with comp. One of the big breakthroughs I want you guys to have is thinking about marketing to get salespeople the same way you do marketing to get prospects. I'll touch on these. I'll give it to you. I'll give it to you briefly. Many of you guys know the lifetime gross profit per employee to the cost of acquiring talent. Let me explain. Let's say I need another HVAC technician. An HVAC technician costs me, let's say 150 a year. I know they don't cost that. Just go with me. They make 150 a year and they're going to bring in, I don't know, 400K in revenue. That means that net-net, I'm going to make 250K in gross profit, 250K per year in gross profit for my talent. Then the question is, if I told you you have an investment and say, make you $250,000 a year in gross profit, what would you be willing to pay for this? I just said, this is what I've got. I've got investments. Great thing. It's already proven. I've got four other ones. They all make $250,000 a year in gross profit. What would you be willing to pay? It does it every year. What would you be willing to pay for it? 50K? Okay, cool. Here's the crazy thing. I go through this exercise with tons of people in home services or business owners who are looking for salespeople and they're like, oh yeah, I would pay 50K for that. I'm like, right, this is the cost of acquiring talent. Are you willing to pay $50,000 in marketing and recruiting to get a good salesperson? The math, maths. People are so afraid to do this. When we built one of our sales teams up, we went from zero to, was it 50 guys? Yeah, 50 or 60 activated. So I think we hired like 90 and then cut down to 50 or 60. We did that in 90 days and people are like, how did you do it? I was like, oh, we just paid 10 different recruiting firms, five or 10,000 ahead for each guy, and then we spun up our own recruiting process too. I think it was like 50, 50. We got half and the other recruiting firms got the other half. So it's like, what did we pay all in? I don't know. At the worst case, we paid 250 grand all in, but then I mean, anybody who's made any money, if you pay $250,000 and got a 50 person sales team, that team generated $5 million a month in sales. What are we talking about? Right? And so don't be cheap with your growth. Like you're stealing from tomorrow for today. So just do the math. Okay. This was my, this was my reduced presentation. All right. I'm sorry. My apologies, everyone. So I was like, I want to make sure I provide enough. Okay. Thank you, Ben. I appreciate it. Okay. So if you have a high volume role like SDRs, you need to always be interviewing. It's something a big mistake people make to get into this, this flow of like on off, like if you have a high volume role, you're going to have turn, you should always be interviewing. Okay. That also means that if you have a high volume role, you need to change your interview process. You need to have groups screening rather than one on one. It's not efficient for you to do one on one calls when you're talking to like low, low level. When I say low level, I'm not talking about their human essence. I'm talking about their skill. All right. And so this is the process that we follow. So it's somebody applies. We send them the script. If we like them, we get a video back from them with them saying the script, which you can get a 60 seconds. So you need to say the intro, say the whatever, right? We invite them to a group interview. We do a one to two minute role play with a bunch of people. So one to two, one to two, one to two, one to two, one to two, all together. We give them feedback after the role play. They, if they do a good job, we invite them to a follow up call. We job offer. That's how it works. If you need a superstar closer, that's not what this, this would be an SDR process. Superstar closer, you're probably going to do one on one outreach and bring them in and have a one on one call. And again, everyone starts as low man closures get earmarked to ascend faster, but they have to, they still have to get the same KPIs because everyone needs to be able to hunt. Now, I'll bring this up. This is our career path for our salespeople. And I like to bring this up because like, I think every single, almost every one of you guys, especially if you want to build a sales team. So here's like, can I get a one in the chat if you actually are building a sales team? All right, then I'll bring this up. Okay. I was like, if no one's doing it, then I'm not going to go to this detail. All right. So I think about it like this. So it's like you're like, so these are just placeholders. All right, but fundamentally, this is like a night like a progress matrix, right? So it's like, you have a junior, you have the actual title, and then you have a senior. So it'd be like junior, SDR, SDR, senior SDR, right? And you could see our career path there, right? Junior SDR, SDR, senior SDR, SDR manager, then junior closer, closer, senior closer, closer manager, and then they, and then they can, from closer manager, they can go to any of the other kind of career paths. But the reason I find this important, and you guys, you like, it took me a long time to figure this out. Especially when you have roles that are repeated roles like support, editors, salespeople, like these are roles that you have a lot of people in. You want to have multiple tiers of titling, and you can have small changes in comps so that people have regular kind of like reinforcement things to look forward to, right? Because if, fundamentally, the roles that are like this typically have a lot of repetition. And so having some sort of like break in that repetition with just small tweaks in title and comp can make a huge like difference. And so you could literally go junior, comp change, normal SDR, comp change, senior SDR, that gives you five increments of reward. And so like every 12 weeks, they can get like an improvement. It could be minor, but it's just enough that like, they feel like they're in it. And this creates a career path. And so you want to be able to explain this career path to someone. What are these titles means in terms of responsibility? Honestly, Miles, a lot of it is just about cumulative reps. It's like once you close your first 20 deals, you go from junior to SDR, and it comes with a comp change, right? Or you go from junior SDR to slightly higher pay, you keep the same title when you get your first 25. At 50 deals, you go from junior to SDR. And then you go from 50 to 100, you get a comp change, you still SDR. At 200, you go from SDR to senior SDR. And maybe you get better hours and you get better leads, right? So there's always like little things you can do. Like if I was editors, they'd get better content if I had to pick. If I had sales guys, they get better leads or better hours, right? There's always like tiny little perks you can give somebody for those benefits that come with title. But basically junior, the name senior, and then if you want manager, which would be a fourth kind of title, but we're big on like, I don't want everyone to think they need to be a manager because they're just the numbers don't work. Being able to go from senior SDR to junior closer is a great jump for a lot of people if they earn it. Now, one of the key points that I'll make in terms of career path is that at any one of these career paths, like if you hit an inflection point, like your senior SDR, or you're a senior closer, or even a closer at any of these levels, once you hit the KPI, it means you are qualified for a promotion, not entitled to a promotion. So once you hit 50, it doesn't mean it's going to happen. You still have to like be a great contributor to the team and be a cultural fit and all these other things. So that way it's not like I never want anyone on this team to feel like they deserve or entitled to anything. Like everything is earned, period. So junior SDR, SDR, senior SDR, cool, next step, junior closer, closer, senior closer. Hopefully this is guys giving you guys a lot of incremental changes that you can make in a role that's otherwise relatively repetitive, that gives people career markers so they feel like they're making progress. Like think about what they're going to go home and say to their wife or go to say to their parents of like, hey, I got a promotion. I was like, hey, Johnny's doing well at work. It's like, hey, he keeps moving up and like they feel like they're making progress. And so if you just go from junior SDR to SDR manager and then closer manager, and it takes two years between each jump, you're going to lose people. So you got to have those little, you got to have something in eyesight of like in line of sight that they can move, they can look towards. Yeah, sweet. Okay. How long do you want to take for an average performer? Good question. What is it? Six months or 12 months? What is it for the SDRs that we haven't lined up for? Target like time? To promotion? Yeah, for like, it's like six months. It's per or per leap. So we try to have the first couple of leaps happen faster. Yeah, locked into it. So the way that we, yeah, the way that we design this is kind of on reinforcement loops. So especially when people are newer, we want people to get a quick win within the first six to 12 weeks. And basically, if you look at human behavior, a reinforcement cycle, if you want to like have someone be addicted, by the way, this is how you do it. So let's say that someone starts, you want the first loop of reinforcement, the second loop of reinforcement can be twice as long, the third loop of reinforcement can be three times so long. So it's like, you just need these intervals of reinforcement to expand with time. And so this should be like, you go from junior, so you're junior here, and this makes you into an SDR. SDR becomes senior SDR here. Senior SDR becomes junior closer here. Right. And so each of these, you'd be like, okay, maybe this is, you know, six to 12 weeks. So so this is the last thing in our closure manual that I very proud of. I wrote it. So I guess, anyways, this kind of embodies the culture we have. And so, yeah. So what being the top 0.01% looks like? People want what you have, not what you did to get it. Jimmy Carr. February 2010. And so, yeah, I think we had a lot of time to get it. In the fall of every year, we would rush freshmen to join our fraternity. And every year they'd begin pledging. They'd go from being courted, entertained, everyone learning their names, being made to feel special to overnight, quite literally, being told to pick up the trash from their own parties, being treated poorly, and generally suffering. And every year, like clockwork, within two weeks of pledging starting, the entire class would have it. The year I was president of the fraternity, the same thing happened. The pledge class tried to quit on us because pledging wasn't what they thought it would be. So they asked me to talk to them as a group across campus at the freshman dorms, their home turf. I knocked on the door with my VP and entered a hot four person dorm room with 24 guys crammed into it. Four guys on the edge of every bed and every wall spaced taken up and every college foodstown covered in 19 year old frustrated guy. I said, so what's up? All at once they began shouting their grievances. This isn't what we were promised. Yeah, that's not, this isn't right. We shouldn't have to be doing, I have to focus on school. After a minute, it died down. I asked them, forget the pledging process. Who here wants to be a part of this fraternity or at least wanted to be? Slowly, they all reluctantly raised their hands. I was like, okay, good. Now, something like what we have here is pretty cool, right? Silent nods of agreement. Now, when you heard our reputation, you thought it was cool that we were so tightly knit, right? And you wanted to be part of something like that, right? More nods of agreement. Yeah, but this isn't that. Someone piped up. I raised my hand and acknowledged them. I was like, okay, right. So who here thought the pledging process would be easy? No one raised their hand. Then I want you to sit in this moment for a second. I want you to think about all the reasons you don't want to keep going, all the very reasonable things that you're giving up now for something unknown later. Really think about it. I pause for a few seconds to let the moment sink in and I scan the room to meet their eyes. This, this is what hard feels like. I continued, this feeling you have that you don't want to keep going, that you have all these reasons. Some of you have never experienced this before. This is it. Now you know. This is what hard feels like. And the gift of this is that you now get to decide what type of man you want to be, the type he gives up when it's hard or the types who gets even harder. I scan the room. They were back in. I stayed for a few moments to tell them it was okay. Then I went on my way, but I never forgot that moment. I tell this story because hard is relative. And I would encourage you to think of the many hard things you've been through in your life and use them to remind yourself that you can do this. You're going to sacrifice some now for more later. And if you jump ship too soon, you miss out on all the best parts. We don't put you through this process because we want to make you suffer. If anything far from it, I want you to be the best. But unfortunately the part, but unfortunately the process of helping someone become the best forces them to kill the part of them that's their worst. And that part is painful. Just remember that you're not alone. Every person on the team went through exactly what you're about to go through and is better for it. If anything, what you're about to enter will become the memories that you will refer back to as the good old days. With that said, this is what hard feels like and it's worth it. Now many people say they want to be in the top 1% or 0.1% or even 0.01%. But saying that has zero bearing on whether it happens. Achievement comes from actions, not aspirations. So let's get real. To be the top 1%, you need to enter a room of 100 people and leave number one. To be the top 0.1%, you need to enter a room, a high school stadium, high school arena, basketball court and leave number one. If you want to be the top 0.01%, you need to enter a stadium, an arena of 10,000 people and leave number one. A true stadium filled with humans. And in a battle to the death in that stadium, you have to come out on top. You beat everyone. Not almost everyone, everyone. And if you have the goal to be the top 0.1%, do you think that you can live a normal life? Do you think that you can keep the same friends? Do you think you can keep the same hobbies? Do you think you can stay up late and sleep in on weekends? Do you think you don't have to sacrifice what average people care about? Do you think they will support you when you start to pass them? Do you think anyone will think this is healthy, balanced or logical? No. And they're right. But it doesn't matter. When you want to be the 0.01%, there's no greater waste of time than explaining yourself to people who actively don't support you. It's normal for people to not understand why you do what you do. I say this because you cannot make yourself exceptional and live a normal life. To make yourself exceptional, you must live an exceptional life. And an exceptional life does not always mean better. It means it's so different that most people reject it and you. And when that happens, you must reject them as well. Oil and water do not mix. That is what it really means to be exceptional. You must become the exception. So I routinely get asked the secret to success and I believe it just comes down to this. Number one, get better. Number two, never stop. If you do only those two things, you'll win. The problem is people convince themselves they no longer want something once they experience how hard it is to get. So I want to set this expectation as you head off to practice scripts, mark your calendar and set your alarms. The work begins when your motivation ends. Just win, Alex. So I'll leave you guys with that. That's my final word to the sales team when they get hired. That's what I will leave you guys with. As you practice your scripts and as you tell your team and you've reset the cultures that you have, we play to win here. I think that comes from the top. That comes from you. I don't think you can fake it.
